question: [File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=eq:X` filter for a token ID that the account is not associated with, causing the IN clause to include that ID, and if `getInClauseSubQuery()` does not properly parameterize the value, inject SQL that reorganizes the result set to include token relationships from other accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `last(tokens)` call that retrieves the last token in the current page to generate the `nextLink`, by crafting a request where the last token ID is manipulated via filter conditions, causing the next page link to point to a position that skips or repeats records in the history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getCachedTokens() in tokenService.js] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `tokenCache.set()` is called without any TTL or expiration, causing token metadata (freeze_status, kyc_status, decimals) to remain cached indefinitely, so that after a token update, all history responses continue to show the pre-update state, reorganizing the apparent timeline of token status changes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=gt:X` filter where X is set to the maximum possible token ID minus one, causing the query to return only the very last token association in the history, making it appear that the account has only one token relationship, hiding all earlier associations?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: extractTokensRelationshipQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=lte:X` filter combined with `order=desc` to cause the query to return token relationships in descending order up to token ID X, presenting a reorganized view where the most recently associated tokens appear first within the filtered range?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=gte:X` filter where X is set to a value that excludes the first N token associations, combined with `order=asc`, to make it appear that the account's token relationship history starts at a later point than it actually does, hiding the earliest associations?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery() in tokenService.js] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=eq:X` parameter where X contains a SQL comment sequence (e.g., `100--`) that, when interpolated directly into `in (${tokenIdInParams})`, comments out the rest of the SQL query, causing the ORDER BY or LIMIT clause to be dropped and reorganizing the returned token history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=eq:X` parameter where X is a very large number that exceeds the valid token ID range, causing the IN clause to include an out-of-range value that either errors or causes the query to return unexpected results, reorganizing the visible token history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=gt:X` filter where X is set to a value just below a gap in the token ID sequence, causing the query to skip over the gap and return only token relationships after the gap, making it appear that the account's history is continuous when it actually has missing associations?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: extractTokensRelationshipQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply both `token.id=gte:X` (range condition pushed to `conditions`) and `token.id=eq:Y` (pushed to `inConditions`) simultaneously, causing the SQL query to combine a range filter with an IN clause in a way that produces an intersection that excludes valid token relationships, reorganizing the visible history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=lte:X` filter where X is set to a value just below the token ID of a specific association, causing that association to be excluded from the response, hiding it from the history without any indication that records were omitted?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTokenAccounts() in tokenService.js] Can an unprivileged external user cause the `rows.map()` call that creates `TokenAccount` objects to process rows in a different order than the SQL ORDER BY clause specifies, if the JavaScript runtime reorders the array, causing the returned token history to be reorganized relative to the database order?
